title: Security comments throughout with clear rationale
description:
The codebase uses SECURITY: comments to flag security-critical code paths with clear explanations. Examples:

**lora_l2.c:134-138 (EUI-64 derivation):**
```c
/* SECURITY: Refusing to start without stable identity. A random EUI-64
 * would change on each reboot, breaking IPv6 NDP and mesh routing. */
```

**lora_l2.c:239-254 (buffer overflow detection):**
```c
/*
 * SECURITY: If driver returned more than buffer size, stack corruption
 * has ALREADY occurred. rx_buf is stack-allocated, so out-of-bounds
 * writes may have corrupted the return address...
 */
```

**lichen_l2.c:82-96 (require LICHEN_LINK):**
```c
/*
 * SECURITY: Require LICHEN_LINK for production builds...
 * Without CONFIG_LICHEN_LINK, packets are sent as raw IPv6 over LoRa with:
 * - No MIC/CRC protection (corruption goes undetected)
 * - No SCHC compression (40-byte IPv6 header wastes MTU)
 * - No replay protection
 */
```

**lichen_l2.c:765-774 (key material handling):**
```c
/*
 * SECURITY: Copy link_key into a local buffer rather than capturing a
 * pointer to link_ctx.link_key. This ensures rx_ctx remains valid even
 * if a future refactor moves lichen_link_cleanup() outside the rx_mutex.
 */
```

Each SECURITY comment explains what the threat is, not just what the code does. This is defensive documentation that prevents well-meaning refactors from introducing vulnerabilities.
close_reason: Positive finding - no action needed, documents good practice
